## CADO-NFS 3.0.0-modern

A **modernization + performance fork** of upstream
[CADO-NFS](https://gitlab.inria.fr/cado-nfs/cado-nfs) **3.0.0** — a complete
implementation of the **Number Field Sieve (NFS)** for integer factorization and
discrete logarithms. This release rebases the fork onto the 3.0.0 base, builds
and runs on a current bleeding-edge toolchain (**CMake 4.x, GCC 16, C++20,
Python 3.14**), and adds four tracks of *rigorously measured* performance and
orchestration work.

> **No NFS mathematics is changed.** Every track is an implementation, build, or
> orchestration optimization. Correctness — `make check` plus verified
> `product == N` factorizations — is the gate after each phase. Results below are
> reported honestly, **including the parts that did not pay off.**

**Reference hardware for all measurements:** Intel **i9-10850K** (10C/20T, Comet
Lake → no AVX-512) + **RTX 3090** (Ampere, `sm_86`), 64 GiB, CachyOS.

---

### Highlights

- **~25–32 % less CPU work** than the prior `2.3.1-modern` fork on identical
  inputs (upstream 3.0.0's Bouvier–Imbert batch cofactorization + `I>16` sieving,
  plus `-O3 -march=native`).
- A complete, validated **Rust work-unit client + server** that speaks the exact
  CADO HTTP/JSON protocol and can be **swapped in live** for the Python Flask
  server (TLS, IP-whitelist, cert-pinning; 13/13 robustness tests).
- A working, bit-exact **GPU ECM cofactorization** backend (CUDA) — shipped with
  an **honest negative result**: it yields no net single-machine speedup at these
  sizes (Amdahl-bound), and the analysis explains exactly why.
- An **AVX-512 VPCLMULQDQ** GF(2)[x] kernel for `lingen`, validated bit-exact
  under Intel SDE (performance gated on real AVX-512 silicon).
- Two new **NFS explainers** (plain-English + the full mathematics), GPU and Rust
  **deep-dive docs**, a reproducible **benchmark suite**, and full **CI**.

---

### Performance (c60–c90, all 20 threads, verified)

| Digits | Bits | Wall time | CADO CPU | Parallel | vs 2.3.1-modern (CPU) |
|-------:|-----:|----------:|---------:|---------:|----------------------:|
| 60 | ~199 | 19.2 s | 40.0 s | 2.1× | −31 % |
| 70 | ~232 | 26.0 s | 87.5 s | 3.4× | −28 % |
| 80 | ~265 | 70.3 s | 379.1 s | 5.4× | −32 % |
| 90 | ~299 | 184.5 s | 1465.0 s | 8.0× | −25 % |

The robust, repeatable signal is **total CPU work, down ~25–32 %**. Wall-time
gains shrink with size and fall inside the ±15–20 % polynomial-selection noise by
c90. Parallel efficiency *drops* (c90 11.1×→8.0×) precisely *because* the CPU
fell — less embarrassingly-parallel sieve work makes the sequential phases a
larger fraction. Full methodology, per-phase breakdown, and projections:
[`BENCHMARKS.md`](BENCHMARKS.md).

---

### What changed, by track

#### Phase 1 — Build & compiler *(measured, not guessed)*
- Adopted **`-O3 -march=native -mtune=native`** (host AVX2/BMI2/FMA codegen).
  Measured on a deterministic c120 siever microbenchmark (`bench/las-microbench.sh`):
  `stock -O2` 12.57 s → `-O3 -march=native` 11.66 s (**−7.2 %**).
- **LTO and PGO were evaluated and rejected on evidence** (LTO 0 % and breaks
  `-Werror`; PGO **+2.8 %**, a regression). No guessing — the numbers are in the
  CHANGELOG.

#### Phase 2 — SIMD
- **AVX2 on the siever was ruled out by profiling** — the hot path is scatter
  (`fill_in_buckets`, small-sieve) plus scalar modular arithmetic, not
  vectorizable loops; the explicitly-SSE2 survivor code is cold.
- Added an **AVX-512 VPCLMULQDQ** GF(2)[x] base-case kernel (the `lingen` hot
  path) — four carryless multiplies per instruction vs one per 128-bit `pclmul`.
  **Validated bit-exact over 200 000 random trials under Intel SDE.** Performance
  is gated on real AVX-512 hardware (the reference box is Comet Lake), so this is
  shipped correctness-validated, not perf-claimed.

#### Phase 3 — GPU ECM cofactorization *(honest negative result)*
- A complete batched CUDA ECM backend (`sieve/ecm/`): 64-bit **and** 128-bit
  Montgomery ladders (CIOS REDC), **stage-2 BSGS**, **Suyama-σ** curves, on-device
  GCD, per-thread streams with `cudaMallocAsync`. The same `__host__ __device__`
  code runs on CPU and GPU, so the GPU path is **bit-exact**.
- The inner modmul primitive hits **~279 G/s on the RTX 3090 vs ~7.2 G/s across 20
  CPU cores (~39×)**.
- **Honest conclusion:** cofactorization is only ~8 % of siever time (Amdahl
  ceiling), and a mid-effort "throughput win" turned out to be a **correctness
  bug** (a single-factor batch emitting relations with primes > `lpb`). With
  correctness enforced there is **no net single-machine speedup** at these sizes;
  in the heavy `mfb1=90` regime the corrected batch is ~2.3× slower because
  `facul` already finds every valid relation. Documented in full as a *measured
  negative*, not an unsubstantiated win: [`docs/gpu-cofactorization.md`](docs/gpu-cofactorization.md).

#### Phase 4 — Rust orchestration *(robustness / scalability)*
- **`cado-nfs-client-rs`** (reqwest + rustls, no OpenSSL): the full client loop —
  fetch work-unit, download + checksum-verify (sha1/256/3-256), run commands (no
  shell), upload results — plus multi-server failover, TLS cert-pinning
  (`--certsha1`), `--niceness`, download `flock`, and `STDIN<n>` redirection (which
  the stock Python client never implemented).
- **`cado-wu-server-rs`** (axum + tokio + bundled rusqlite): the five endpoints
  over the same `wudb` SQLite schema and status codes, with stale-work timeout
  reassignment, `410`-after-finish, an r2d2/WAL connection pool, TLS, and IP-
  whitelist enforcement (IPv4/IPv6 exact + CIDR).
- **In-process swap:** set `CADO_RUST_WU_SERVER=<binary>` and a normal
  `cado-nfs.py` run uses the Rust server in place of Flask, over the same DB —
  validated end-to-end over plain HTTP *and* TLS (cert-pinning + whitelist).
- **Validation:** `interop-test.sh`, `server-interop-test.sh`,
  `robustness-test.sh` (**13/13**), `deploy-test.sh` (a real 59-digit
  factorization run entirely by external Rust clients), and the two swap tests.
  Full detail: [`docs/rust-orchestration.md`](docs/rust-orchestration.md). The
  high-level task DAG (`cadotask.py`) is deliberately left in Python.

---

### Quick start

```bash
# 1) one-time: create the Flask/requests venv the 3.0.0 orchestrator needs
bash scripts/setup-venv.sh

# 2) build (out-of-tree into build/<hostname>/, using local.sh's -O3 -march=native)
make

# 3) factor a number (run cado-nfs.py under the venv python)
cado-nfs.venv/bin/python3 ./cado-nfs.py \
  90377629292003121684002147101760858109247336549001090677693 -t 4
```

**Requirements:** C99 + **C++20** (GCC ≥ 10 / Clang ≥ 12), GMP ≥ 5 built
`--enable-shared`, CMake ≥ 3.18, Python 3 + Flask + requests. Optional: hwloc,
MPI, CUDA (the GPU backend, `-DENABLE_GPU=ON`).

---

### What's in the box

| Area | Files |
|------|-------|
| Theory | [`docs/number-field-sieve-plain-english.md`](docs/number-field-sieve-plain-english.md), [`docs/number-field-sieve.md`](docs/number-field-sieve.md) |
| Deep-dives | [`docs/gpu-cofactorization.md`](docs/gpu-cofactorization.md), [`docs/rust-orchestration.md`](docs/rust-orchestration.md) |
| Perf / changes | [`BENCHMARKS.md`](BENCHMARKS.md), [`CHANGELOG.md`](CHANGELOG.md) |
| Rust workspace | `rust/cado-nfs-client`, `rust/cado-wu-server`, `rust/*-test.sh` |
| GPU backend | `sieve/ecm/gpu_ecm.cu`, `gpu_cofac.cpp`, `config/cuda.cmake`, `bench/gpu-*.cu` |
| Project / community | `CLAUDE.md`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`, `SECURITY.md`, `CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md`, CI + issue/PR templates |

The attached `cado-nfs-3.0.0-modern.tar.gz` is the complete tracked source tree
(built with `git archive`); verify it with the accompanying `.sha256`. GitHub's
auto-generated "Source code" archives are equivalent.

---

### Verification

This release is published only after a CI job rebuilds the tagged tree on a
clean Ubuntu runner, runs the `ctest` fast subset, and factors the 59-digit
reference number checking **product == N** and primality of each factor. Locally,
the full `make check` suite (~795 tests) and seeded c60–c90 factorizations pass.

### Compatibility & honesty notes

- AVX-512 kernels are **runtime-optional and correctness-validated only** here —
  the reference CPU is Comet Lake. Performance claims for them await AVX-512
  hardware.
- The GPU backend is **off by default** (`-DENABLE_GPU=OFF`); enabling it does not
  change the CPU result, and — per the documented Amdahl analysis — it is not a
  single-machine speedup at these sizes.
- This is **not** the official CADO-NFS. The earlier 2.3.0-based release is the
  `v2.3.1-modern` tag.
